What a week it's been... I'm dealing with tendonitis in my right shoulder, specifically under my shoulder blade. Whenever I overuse it, particularly with computer work, the pain is intense. At this point, I can hardly move my arm because I spent a lot of time updating my website and tackling other online tasks. Drawing takes much more effort now, and the lack of sleep only worsens the situation. While I'm not in the best spirits at the moment, I'm confident I'll recover soon. On a brighter note, my mom's surgery went well. Her eyesight is gradually returning; she can now discern fuzzy shapes and identify colors, although she remains significantly visually impaired. We're hopeful that she'll fully regain her vision soon.

Podcasts mark rise of diy radio an apple ipod or other digital music players can hold anything up to 10,000 songs, which is a lot of space to fill. But more and more ipod owners are filling that space with audio content created by an unpredictable assortment of producers. It is called "podcasting" and its strongest proponent is former mtv host and vj (video jockey) adam curry. Podcasting takes its name from the apple ipod, although you do not need an ipod to create one or to listen to a podcast. A podcast is basically an internet-based radio show which podcasters create, usually in the comfort of their own home. They need only a microphone, a pc, and some editing software. They then upload their shows to the internet and others can download and listen to them, all for free.

While I wouldn't personally recommend this game, it wasn't entirely awful either. The gunplay holds up well, as long as you have the funds to buy gun parts. This highlights my primary issue with the game: it's heavily reliant on a pay-to-win model. If I could overlook that aspect, the game would actually be quite enjoyable. The hardsuit is a blast to use, and the HRV feature is a fantastic concept. I would choose this game over any Call of Duty title anytime, but its pay-to-win microtransaction approach is discouraging for me. If you're comfortable with this model, feel free to enjoy it. However, if you're like me and dislike pay-to-win games, it's best to pass on this one; it simply isn't suited to our preferences.

As the clock ticks down to the midnight deadline on February 1, Fiat and General Motors (GM) are racing against time to resolve their disagreement over a potential takeover. The 2000 agreement, which granted Fiat the right to sell its car division to GM, is now at risk of expiring. GM, struggling to stem losses in its European operations, has reconsidered its interest in acquiring the unprofitable Fiat unit. The stalemate in talks has sent Fiat's shares plummeting 1.2% on Tuesday, following a 4% surge on Monday on hopes of a deal being reached. GM is reportedly offering approximately $2 billion (£1.06 billion) to extricate itself from the arrangement, citing Fiat's decision to divest its finance arm and reduce GM's stake through a capital-raising initiative as grounds for nullifying the original agreement. The 2000 deal was forged in a high-stakes game of musical chairs, with GM and DaimlerChrysler vying for a partnership with Fiat.

Embark on a nostalgic journey with this retro-style Transformers game, reminiscent of the beloved 5-minute cartoon series that captivated the hearts of 30-somethings in the UK. The iconic voice actors behind Optimus, Megatron, Bumblebee, and other beloved characters return, instantly recognizable and nostalgic. The distinctive sound design, reminiscent of the classic cartoon, is a masterclass in evoking a bygone era. Visually, the cell-shaded graphics are a triumph, transporting players to a bygone generation. But beyond its aesthetic appeal, the game's combat mechanics are a true standout. The seamless integration of mid-fight transformations, woven into fluid combos, elevates the gameplay experience, setting it apart from other Transformers titles. Notably, the emphasis on melee combat is a refreshing change of pace, while certain enemies, such as the Seekers, require a strategic shift to ranged attacks, adding depth to the overall experience.

The proliferation of podcasts has revolutionized the world of radio broadcasting, offering a unique platform for individuals to create and disseminate their own audio content. With the capacity to store up to 10,000 songs, digital music players such as the Apple iPod have inadvertently paved the way for a new wave of amateur radio producers. This phenomenon, dubbed "podcasting," has gained significant traction, thanks in part to the tireless efforts of its most ardent advocate, Adam Curry, a former MTV host and video jockey. Despite its name, podcasting is not exclusive to the iPod; rather, it is a form of internet-based radio that can be created and consumed by anyone with a microphone, a computer, and basic editing software. From the comfort of their own homes, podcasters can produce and upload their shows to the internet, where they can be freely downloaded and enjoyed by a global audience.

Taking a deep breath in and out... So, music wasn't always my profession. For nearly five years after earning my degree from a prestigious liberal arts college, this was how I made ends meet. *chuckles* I worked as an independent performance artist—the "Eight-Foot Bride," a living statue. I adore sharing this part of my past because people are invariably curious about the real lives behind such unconventional characters.  

Here's how it worked: I'd cover myself in white paint, perch on a pedestal with a hat or collection tin at my feet. When a passerby dropped in a coin, I'd offer them a flower—along with a piercing, meaningful gaze. And if they declined the flower? I'd respond with a silent, wistful gesture of heartache as they moved on. *laughs*

Halloween writer debra hill dies screenwriter and producer debra hill, best known for her work on the 70s horror classic halloween, has died in los angeles aged 54. Hill, who had been suffering from cancer, co-wrote the 1978 film, which starred jamie lee curtis as a babysitter terrorised by a psychopath. Directed by john carpenter, it made over $60m (£31.3m) worldwide - a record for independent film at that time. Hill also worked with carpenter on escape from new york and the fog. Born in new jersey, hill began her career as a production assistant and worked her way through the ranks, becoming an assistant director and second-unit director before she began collaborating with carpenter. She was regarded by many as a pioneering woman in film, taking on jobs in the 70s that were more commonly taken by men.

When random effects are correlated with sample design variables, the typical practice of using individual survey weights—designed to be inversely proportional to unit survey inclusion probabilities—to form a pseudo-likelihood no longer leads to asymptotically unbiased inference. We propose a weight-exponentiated formulation for the random effects distribution that enables unbiased inference for generating the hyperparameters of the random effects. In comparison to frequentist methods that depend on numerical integration, our Bayesian approach is unique in achieving both unbiased estimation relative to the sampling design distribution and consistency with the population-generating distribution. Through simulations and a real-world example involving a survey of business establishments, we demonstrate the effectiveness of our method across various modeling frameworks and sampling designs. This work culminates recent advancements combining traditional survey estimation techniques with the Bayesian modeling paradigm, effectively bridging these two rich yet distinct subfields.

My gaming experience with this game was marked by a significant amount of time spent, with a total of 8.6 hours dedicated to reaching its conclusion. Throughout my journey, I discovered a total of 10 collectibles out of a possible 25, and unfortunately, I did not achieve any of the 15 available achievements. The aspects that I enjoyed the most were the visuals, which remained impressive until the very end, particularly the ice caves. While the story itself was not particularly remarkable, the ending, however, left a lasting impression, making the time I invested in playing this game feel worthwhile. On the other hand, the visuals in the final level were underwhelming, and the gameplay became increasingly frustrating as it became necessary to repeat the same tasks numerous times before achieving success. Moreover, the achievements were extremely challenging, with only 1.7% of players, including myself, managing to achieve more than 2 of the 15 available.

Roxy Music is set to perform at the Isle of Wight Music Festival this June, sharing the stage with Morrissey, Supergrass, and Idlewild. REM has already been confirmed as the headliner for the three-day event, scheduled for June 10-12. Original band members Bryan Ferry, Phil Manzanera, Andy Mackay, and Paul Thompson will perform on the festival's Saturday night. The band is also in the studio working on new material, their first since the release of their 1982 album, Avalon. Roxy Music was formed in 1971 by Ferry, Mackay, and Brian Eno, with Manzanera joining the group the following year. They achieved their first hit with "Virginia Plain" in 1972, followed by other well-known tracks such as "Street Life," "Love Is the Drug," and their 1981 cover of John Lennon's "Jealous Guy.

You’ve been accused of a crime you did not commit. It’s impossible to prove your innocence. If you insist that you’re innocent anyway, you’ll likely be found guilty and executed. But if you confess, apologize, and implicate others for good measure, you’ll go free. Do you give a false confession— or risk a public hanging?this was the choice facing those accused of witchcraft in the village of salem, massachusetts between february 1692 and may 1693. They were the victims of paranoia about the supernatural, misdirected religious fervor— and a justice system that valued repentance over truth.salem was settled in 1626 by puritans, a group of english protestants. Life was strict and isolated for the people of salem. Battles with their native american neighbors and groups of french settlers were commonplace.

**Revised Version:**  

While I previously defended parents—even leaning toward their perspective—I’ve since reconsidered. The truth is, parents often believe they understand us completely, convinced they can decipher every emotion we display. But they don’t. In reality, they know very little about who we truly are.  

Take tonight, for example. After arguing with Nicole about Kelli, I retreated upstairs, only to be met with intrusive questions about what was wrong. Honestly? I don’t owe them an explanation for everything. The situation is simple: Nicole and Kelli can’t stand each other. That’s all there is to it.

Deep learning has solved many problems that are out of reach of heuristic algorithms. It has also been successfully applied in wireless communications, even though the current radio systems are well-understood and optimal algorithms exist for many tasks. While some gains have been obtained by learning individual parts of a receiver, a better approach is to jointly learn the whole receiver. This, however, often results in a challenging nonlinear problem, for which the optimal solution is infeasible to implement. To this end, we propose a deep fully convolutional neural network, deeprx, which executes the whole receiver pipeline from frequency domain signal stream to uncoded bits in a 5g-compliant fashion. We facilitate accurate channel estimation by constructing the input of the convolutional neural network in a very specific manner using both the data and pilot symbols.

The concept of money has been a fascinating subject throughout history, and I'd like to explore its evolution by examining a unique culture that existed in Micronesia during the early 1900s, known as the Yap. The Yap people are particularly intriguing due to their unique monetary system, which revolves around limestone discs called Rai stones. Unlike our modern currencies, these stones are not physically moved or exchanged, as they can grow quite massive in size. The largest Rai stone measures approximately four tons and 12 feet in diameter. To keep track of ownership, the Yap people simply keep a record of who possesses a specific portion of the stone. Interestingly, there's a story about a group of sailors who were transporting a massive stone across the ocean when they encountered some adversity, and the stone actually fell into the sea.

Density-based clustering is grounded in the notion that clusters are inherently linked to distinct characteristics of the underlying probability distribution that governs the data. By acknowledging the existence of a true, albeit unknown, population structure, this approach enables the clustering problem to be framed within a conventional inferential framework. Here, the concept of ideal population clustering is rigorously defined as the partition that arises from the true density function. A nonparametric variant of this methodology, known as modal clustering, establishes a direct correspondence between clusters and the domains of attraction associated with density modes. The operationalization of this approach necessitates the estimation of a nonparametric density function, with the judicious selection of the smoothing parameter being crucial in shaping the density landscape and, by extension, the modal structure that underlies the final clustering partition. This study undertakes an asymptotic examination of density estimation in the context of modal clustering, with a focus on elucidating the theoretical foundations that govern this process.

The paper "Baryonic feedback and the cosmic shear power spectrum: A new approach" by E. Bulbul, et al. aims to address the impact of baryonic feedback on the cosmic shear power spectrum, which is a key tool for studying the large-scale structure of the universe. The authors use simulations to generate a mock data set for the cosmic shear power spectrum and its covariance matrix, which are then corrected for baryonic effects using the baryonification method of Schneider et al. (2019). They also develop an emulator to quickly predict the baryonic power suppression, allowing them to perform a likelihood analysis for a standard $\Lambda$CDM cosmology with both cosmological and astrophysical parameters.

The paper starts by discussing the importance of baryonic feedback in the universe, particularly in the context of cosmic shear surveys. The authors explain that baryonic feedback can lead to a suppression of the cosmic shear power spectrum on small scales, which can have a significant impact on the accuracy of weak lensing surveys. They note that current weak lensing surveys are limited by the poorly constrained shape and amplitude of this suppression, which can lead to uncertainties in the derived cosmological parameters.

Where does all the carbon we release go? carbon works in a natural cycle, present in all living things: in the soil, in the oceans, in the atmosphere and in rocks deep underground. When plants and other living things die, much of their stored carbon makes its way back to the atmosphere until new plants grow and reabsorb the carbon.but over millions of years, some of the carbon stored in ancient trees and sea life becomes rock and some becomes fossil fuels: coal, oil and natural gas. Natural events like volcanic eruptions release some of the carbon trapped in rock. And human activities, like burning fossil fuels, also release some of that prehistoric carbon.today, humans release around 60 times more carbon than all the volcanoes on the planet each year.

Scott Richter, the self-proclaimed "spam king" and founder of optinrealbig.com, is seeking bankruptcy protection as the company, which has been battling multiple legal battles, including a high-profile lawsuit against Microsoft, faces the possibility of being forced to cease operations. Optinrealbig was listed as the third largest spammer in the world by junk mail watchdog Spamhaus and was sued in December 2003 for sending millions of spam messages that violated anti-spam laws. The company's legal troubles began when it was accused of sending billions of spam messages through 514 compromised net addresses in 35 countries. Microsoft and New York Attorney General Eliot Spitzer brought the lawsuit, alleging that Mr. Richter and his accomplices sent billions of spam messages that violated anti-spam laws. The company's decision to file for Chapter 11 bankruptcy protection would allow it to resolve its legal issues while continuing to operate.

According to recent research, high-speed broadband is significantly empowering individuals to express themselves online. A study by the UK think-tank Demos reveals that one in four broadband users in Britain regularly uploads content and maintains personal websites. The report highlights that the availability of constant, fast internet connections is transforming how people engage with the web. Currently, over five million households in the UK are equipped with broadband, and this figure continues to rise rapidly. The Demos report investigates the influence of broadband on users' online behaviors, discovering that more than half of broadband users access the internet before having breakfast. Moreover, one in five users confessed to waking up in the middle of the night specifically to browse the web. The report further emphasizes that broadband is motivating individuals to take a more engaged and participative approach to the online world.

*[To the global community,]  
[We confront a crisis unlike any in human history.]  
[Now, a vital message from...]  
[United Nations Secretary-General António Guterres.]*  

The climate crisis is the paramount issue of our era. Time is slipping away, and we are falling behind. Across the world—particularly among the youth—frustration mounts over the lack of decisive action.  

This demands greater ambition from every corner of society: governments, cities, corporations, investors, and individuals alike. That is why I welcome the launch of **TED Countdown**. The solutions are within our grasp: the science is clear, the technology exists, and the resources are available. Now, we must channel unwavering political will and collective energy to rise to this challenge. The race is on—let us run it together.

A starving orphan begs for a second serving of gruel. A spinster withers away in her worn wedding dress. A stone-hearted miser is tormented by the Ghost of Christmas Past. Even more than a century after his passing, these memorable characters from the works of Charles Dickens remain instantly recognizable. His body of work is so distinctive that it inspired its own adjective. But what elements of Dickens's writing make it so extraordinary? His fiction is rich with suspense, featuring brooding settings, unexpected plot twists, and compelling mysteries. These characteristics kept his readers always yearning for more. Originally, his stories were published in serial form, with a few chapters released at a time in inexpensive literary journals, before later being compiled into books.

Recent observations of the stars Fomalhaut and HD 202628 have revealed eccentric debris rings that defy expectations under the conventional eccentric planet perturbation model, also known as the "pericenter glow" hypothesis. This standard framework assumes that an initially circular and narrow planetesimal belt, located at a semi-major axis a, undergoes a gradual increase in eccentricity to e_f following the dispersal of the gas disc, driven by secular perturbations from an eccentric planet. This process would theoretically yield a belt with a width of 2ae_f. However, a nuanced variation of this scenario suggests that narrower belts can emerge if the planetesimals possess an inherent eccentricity, potentially inherited from earlier planetary perturbations during the gas-rich protoplanetary disc phase. Alternatively, primordial eccentricity could be the result of disc instabilities that amplify eccentricity, eliminating the need for planetary intervention altogether.

In 1964, Bob Dylan, at the pinnacle of his career at the tender age of 23, is hailed as the voice of a generation. His creative output is nothing short of phenomenal, with classic songs pouring out of him at an astonishing rate. However, a small but vocal minority voices a dissenting opinion, accusing Bob Dylan of plagiarism, claiming that he is borrowing liberally from others. Fast-forward to 2004, Brian Burton, aka Danger Mouse, takes on a bold project. He combines the Beatles' iconic "White Album" with Jay-Z's "The Black Album" to create a unique fusion, "The Grey Album". This innovative mashup sparks an immediate online sensation, prompting the Beatles' record label to send out a flurry of cease-and-desist letters, citing "unfair competition and dilution of our valuable property." Interestingly, the outcome is rebranded as a remix, "The Grey Album".

Observations of thermally driven transverse vibration of a photonic crystal waveguide (pcw) are reported. The pcw consists of two parallel nanobeams with a 240 nm vacuum gap between the beams. Models are developed and validated for the transduction of beam motion to phase and amplitude modulation of a weak optical probe propagating in a guided mode (gm) of the pcw for probe frequencies far from and near to the dielectric band edge. Since our pcw has been designed for near-field atom trapping, this research provides a foundation for evaluating possible deleterious effects of thermal motion on optical atomic traps near the surfaces of pcws. Longer term goals are to achieve strong atom-mediated links between individual phonons of vibration and single photons propagating in the gms of the pcw, thereby enabling opto-mechanics at the quantum level with atoms, photons, and phonons.

A difference-in-differences analysis that employs a control group with distinct characteristics from the treatment group is susceptible to biases stemming from historical events that exert disparate impacts on these groups. To mitigate this issue, constructing a more comparable control group by matching a subset of the overall control group to the treatment group can potentially reduce bias. This phenomenon is explored through simulation studies. A case in point is our investigation into the impact of mountaintop removal mining on mortality rates, utilizing a difference-in-differences analysis that leverages the surge in mountaintop removal mining activities following the 1990 Clean Air Act amendments. By creating a more closely matched control group for this analysis, our results yielded a 95% confidence interval that encompasses a range of possible effects, including substantial adverse outcomes, no discernible impact, and minor beneficial effects, thereby underscoring the complexity of the relationship between mountaintop removal mining and mortality rates.

Four Six Nations captains, including Ireland's Brian O'Driscoll, will lead the Northern Hemisphere squad in the IRB Rugby Aid match on March 5, joining forces for a noble cause. I recently had the opportunity to watch Troy with my family, but unfortunately, it failed to leave a lasting impression on me. Despite its promising premise, the film's execution fell short of expectations. The lavish set design, while visually stunning, seemed to overshadow the narrative, resulting in a disjointed tone and underdeveloped themes. The retelling of the classic story lacked the emotional resonance and depth that would have made it truly compelling. Furthermore, the character development was lacking, leaving the audience without a strong emotional investment in the characters' journeys. The score, too, was a disappointment, bearing an uncanny resemblance to the iconic soundtrack of Gladiator. In contrast to Gladiator, which masterfully wove its haunting score into a gripping narrative, Troy's music felt like a pale imitation, failing to elevate the film's emotional impact.

Ordinary differential equation (ode) is a mathematical model used in many application areas such as climatology, bioinformatics, and chemical engineering with its intuitive appeal to modeling. Despite ode's wide usage in modeling, frequent absence of their analytic solutions makes it difficult to estimate ode parameters from the data, especially when the model has lots of variables and parameters. This paper proposes a bayesian ode parameter estimating algorithm which is fast and accurate even for models with many parameters. The proposed method approximates an ode model with a state-space model based on equations of a numeric solver. It allows fast estimation by avoiding computations of a whole numerical solution in the likelihood. The posterior is obtained by a variational bayes method, more specifically, the approximate riemannian conjugate gradient method (honkela et al.

Where does all the carbon we release go? Carbon is a natural part of the Earth's cycle, present in all living things, from the soil to the oceans and deep beneath the ground. When plants and other living things die, much of their stored carbon is released back into the atmosphere, but some of it becomes trapped in rock and sediment. Over millions of years, this trapped carbon is transformed into fossil fuels, such as coal, oil, and natural gas. Natural events like volcanic eruptions also release some of the carbon trapped in rock. In addition, human activities, such as burning fossil fuels, release a significant amount of prehistoric carbon into the atmosphere each year, with humans releasing around 60 times more carbon than all the volcanoes on Earth combined.

What i'm interested in, though, is — is it true? i mean, it's obviously true for emailing at the dinner table or texting while driving or possibly for live tweeting at ted talk, as well. But i'd like to argue that for an important kind of activity, doing two things at once — or three or even four — is exactly what we should be aiming for.look no further than albert einstein. In 1905, he published four remarkable scientific papers.

Ferguson hails Manchester United's resolve in 1-0 win at Aston Villa

Alex Ferguson praised Manchester United's gutsy performance in their 1-0 win at Aston Villa, describing it as their hardest away game of the season. The Old Trafford boss said the match was end-to-end with plenty of good passing, showcasing the team's character and determination.

"It was a fantastic game of football, with both teams creating chances," Ferguson said. "We showed great character and guts, and we weren't going to let Villa take anything from us. I look at that fixture and see that we've already won there, while Arsenal and Chelsea have yet to come and Villa may have some players back when they do."

Ferguson also singled out senior stars Ryan Giggs and Roy Keane, who came off the bench for the injured John O'Shea. "Roy came on and brought a bit of composure to the midfield, which we needed and which no other player has got," he said.

Understanding the evolution of graphs over time is a complex task across fields like bioinformatics, physics, and social networks. In the scenario we examine, graph edges represent either short-term interactions (such as messaging) or long-term structural connections (such as friendship). Often, long-term edges are manually defined by humans, which can be costly to establish and may not always be optimal for subsequent analysis. To address these challenges, we introduce a model that combines temporal point processes with variational autoencoders to understand temporal attention between nodes based on their communication patterns. By using the dynamics of node interactions, this method enhances flexibility and resolves issues related to manually defined edges, as temporal attention influences how features are propagated between nodes.

As a non-native English speaker, I'd like to provide an honest review of this exceptional game, despite any minor linguistic imperfections. I'll break down my assessment into four distinct categories  graphics, controls, sound, and replayability.

Graphics  The game's comic-like style is visually stunning, boasting sleek and detailed visuals with vibrant color schemes. The graphics can be set to 'high' without putting a strain on your system, even on mid-range hardware. I've played the game on an Intel i5 processor with an NVIDIA GeForce 710M graphics card, 8GB of RAM, and Windows 8.1, and it runs smoothly like clockwork.

Controls  The game can be played with a keyboard and mouse, but it's also compatible with an Xbox controller, offering a more immersive gaming experience.

Two hours into this game, and I'm utterly captivated—both by its brilliance and its relentless humor. The gameplay mechanics are polished, the art direction is stunning, and the soundtrack is unexpectedly refined, perfectly complementing the experience. The narrative and world-building are engaging, while the puzzles strike a satisfying balance of challenge and enjoyment.  

Yet, what truly elevates this game above the rest is its masterful comedy. Whether you're a seasoned RPG veteran—familiar with every trope since the genre's inception—or a complete newcomer, the clever jokes, sharp parodies, and nostalgic references will leave you in stitches. Fans of *Final Fantasy VII* or *X*, in particular, will find it irresistible (though I won't spoil why).  

In fact, after just two hours, I had to pause—not out of frustration, but because my cheeks ached from laughing so hard. This game is an absolute gem, and I can't wait to dive back in.

What is a parent? It's a question that is not easily answered. Today, we have a complex definition of parenthood, encompassing adoption, stepfamilies, and surrogate mothers. Many parents face difficult decisions and questions about how to approach conversations with their children. For instance, should they reveal the story of sperm donation to their child? If so, when is the right time to do so, and what words should they use? The term "biological father" is often used to refer to sperm donors, but is it truly an accurate description? As a philosopher and social scientist, I have delved into these questions about the concept of parenthood. However, today, I will share with you what I've learned from speaking with parents and children. I will demonstrate that what matters most in a family is not necessarily the biological ties, but rather the love, care, and commitment that exists between family members, regardless of their family structure.

The direpack package aims to establish a set of modern statistical dimension reduction techniques into the python universe as a single, consistent package. The dimension reduction methods included resort into three categories: projection pursuit based dimension reduction, sufficient dimension reduction, and robust m estimators for dimension reduction. As a corollary, regularized regression estimators based on these reduced dimension spaces are provided as well, ranging from classical principal component regression up to sparse partial robust m regression. The package also contains a set of classical and robust pre-processing utilities, including generalized spatial signs, as well as dedicated plotting functionality and cross-validation utilities. Finally, direpack has been written consistent with the scikit-learn api, such that the estimators can flawlessly be included into (statistical and/or machine) learning pipelines in that framework.

In 1903, the president of the united states took a three-day camping trip in california’s yosemite valley. President theodore roosevelt slept in a grove of towering sequoia trees, camped in a snowstorm, and spent hours talking around the campfire with his host and guide, conservationist john muir. Roosevelt famously loved the outdoors, but muir had invited him there for more than just camping: yosemite was in danger.though yosemite became protected land in 1864, the valley was still at risk for overdevelopment in 1903. It was at the heart of a decades-old struggle to set aside land for both preservation and public use— two goals that were much easier said than done.the battle over yosemite began with the 1849 gold rush, when miners surged west, seeking gold in the sierra nevada foothills.

To the Moon" transcends being just a game; it is an unforgettable experience, ranking among the most impactful of my life. If you found Telltale's "The Walking Dead" to be poignant, prepare yourself—"To the Moon" surpasses it in every way. Never have I felt so crestfallen yet simultaneously fulfilled by a fictional narrative. In my view, this work stands as a pinnacle of storytelling, complemented by an exquisitely charming and fitting soundtrack. Although the controls can be a bit cumbersome and the gameplay isn't its strongest suit, the story is so captivating that these issues seemed insignificant to me. The game also excels in humor, which is seamlessly woven into the narrative without detracting from its profound nature. And, for fans, there are endless references to Doctor Who to be enjoyed.

A significant breakthrough was achieved over the weekend, as Governor Davis and Senator Polanco reached an informal agreement, whereby Senator Polanco has committed to sponsoring the Southern California Edison Memorandum of Understanding (MOU) bill. Notably, Senator Polanco, a Democrat, is currently embroiled in a personal scandal that has severely impacted his political prospects, effectively ending his Senate career. In light of this, he has announced plans to run for Mayor of Los Angeles instead of seeking re-election to the Senate. The bill is anticipated to be introduced during the new extraordinary session, commencing today or tomorrow. Initially, it will be referred to the Senate Energy Committee, chaired by Senator Bowen, who has expressed strong opposition to the MOU. Consequently, the bill is expected to undergo a prolonged period of committee hearings, during which time it may undergo significant changes. As the bill becomes increasingly entangled in committee politics, the likelihood of a Southern California Edison bankruptcy grows.

Last night, the independent Spirit Awards honored the comedy 'Sideways' with a sweep of six awards, including Best Film. The film's director, Alexander Payne, and co-writer, Jim Taylor, took home the awards for Best Director and Best Screenplay, respectively. The film's stars, Paul Giamatti, Thomas Haden Church, and Virginia Madsen, also received acting awards for their outstanding performances. The Spirit Awards, now in their 20th year, celebrate independent films outside the traditional studio system, and are held the day before the Oscars. Other notable winners included Catalina Sandino Moreno, who won Best Actress for her powerful portrayal of a drug smuggler in the Colombian drama 'Maria Full of Grace.' Moreno is also nominated for Best Actress at the Oscars. The Best First Screenplay award went to Joshua Marston for 'Maria Full of Grace,' a gripping and poignant film that explores the complexities of human nature.

When developing statistical models to summarize clinical data for simulation purposes, rigorous validation is essential to ensure these models accurately replicate the observed data. A robust approach involves employing visual predictive checks (VPCs), a three-step methodological framework: First, generating multiple simulated replications of the original study using the candidate model; second, computing key statistical estimates from each simulated dataset; and third, systematically comparing the distribution of these simulated estimates with those derived from the original clinical data. This versatile technique offers a generally applicable and intuitive validation approach. In this context, we specifically examine its implementation for time-to-event analyses, addressing the particular challenge posed by time-varying covariates that become unobservable or cannot be reasonably estimated following an event occurrence.

In this paper, we study the landscape of the population negative log-likelihood function of gaussian mixture models with a general number of components. Due to nonconvexity, there exist multiple local minima that are not globally optimal, even when the mixture is well-separated. We show that all local minima share the same form of structure that partially identifies the component centers of the true mixture, in the sense that each local minimum involves a non-overlapping combination of fitting multiple gaussians to a single true component and fitting a single gaussian to multiple true components. Our results apply to the setting where the true mixture components satisfy a certain separation condition, and are valid even when the number of components is over-or under-specified. For gaussian mixtures with three components, we obtain sharper results in terms of the scaling with the separation between the components.

Imagine if you could plug your brain into a machine that would bring you ultimate pleasure for the rest of your life. If you were given the choice to sign up for that kind of existence, would you? that's the question philosopher robert nozick posed through a thought experiment he called the experience machine. The experiment asks us to consider a world in which scientists have developed a machine that would simulate real life while guaranteeing experiences of only pleasure and never pain. The catch? you have to permanently leave reality behind, but you'll hardly know the difference. Your experiences will be indistinguishable from reality. Life's natural ups and downs will just be replaced with an endless series of ups. Sounds great, right?

**The Silent Burden of Menstruation**  

A single word—menstruation—encapsulates a universal biological process, yet it remains shrouded in centuries of stigma, reduced to a hushed conversation. Why? At this very moment, over 800 million women worldwide are menstruating. This cycle is the very reason humanity exists, yet society treats it as a source of shame.  

The exhaustion is real. It’s in the furtive glances as you slip a pad from its brown paper wrapper, tuck it into your palm, and hurry to the restroom, praying no one notices. It’s in the forced composure during lectures or meetings, masking the searing pain of cramps with a practiced smile. This is the silent burden we carry—a natural process made unnatural by silence.

The processing of spontaneous conversations in real-world environments, as exemplified by child-centered recordings, poses a significant challenge in the field of audio analysis. However, the development of speech processing models capable of handling diverse acoustic conditions would greatly benefit language acquisition research, where the quantity and quality of speech exposure and production in children are of paramount interest. Furthermore, such models would also facilitate early diagnosis and the assessment of remediation outcomes. This paper introduces our approach to designing an open-source neural network architecture for classifying audio segments into distinct vocalization categories, including those produced by the child wearing the recording device, other children, adult males, and adult females. Our methodology involves the compilation of a diverse, multilingual dataset comprising 260 hours of child-centered recordings across 10 languages, providing a comprehensive foundation for model development and training.

I'm struggling to find my footing with investigative reporting, as it challenges my personal values and the comfort zone I've grown accustomed to. The article I'm working on, and its potential repercussions, have left me feeling uneasy and uncertain. I suspect that my reservations stem from a lack of conviction in the project, which hinders my ability to fully immerse myself in the subject matter and tap into the passion that drives inquiry. My upbringing in a country where dissenting voices are silenced, and questioning authority is met with severe consequences, has instilled in me a cautious approach to self-expression. The freedom to speak my mind and challenge the status quo is a luxury I'm still learning to navigate, and it's a far cry from the culture of repression I was raised in. As a result, I've developed a tendency to be more reserved in my interactions, which can sometimes make it difficult for others to connect with me.

Our story begins in 1964, when a 23-year-old Bob Dylan stands at the precipice of musical greatness. Hailed as the defining voice of his generation, Dylan produces timeless anthems with astonishing frequency. Yet amidst this creative outpouring, a vocal minority accuses the young troubadour of appropriating others' musical work. 

Fast forward to 2004: Producer Brian Burton, better known as Danger Mouse, makes waves by blending The Beatles' iconic "White Album" with Jay-Z's "The Black Album" to create the groundbreaking "Grey Album." This innovative mashup ignites the digital landscape, drawing both widespread acclaim and legal fire from The Beatles' label, which floods the internet with cease-and-desist orders citing "unfair competition and brand dilution." At its core, this controversy revolves around a simple truth: "The Grey Album" represents the art of remixing pushed to bold new frontiers.

It is a physically and emotionally draining experience, often marked by the constant need to conceal evidence of its presence. Imagine having to surreptitiously dispose of a brown paper bag, hastily stuffing it into your pocket, and rushing to the bathroom, all while attempting to maintain a sense of normalcy. Broadband fuels online expression fast web access is encouraging more people to express themselves online, research suggests. A quarter of broadband users in britain regularly upload content and have personal sites, according to a report by uk think-tank demos. It said that having an always-on, fast connection is changing the way people use the internet. More than five million households in the uk have broadband and that number is growing fast. The demos report looked at the impact of broadband on people's net habits. It found that more than half of those with broadband logged on to the web before breakfast. One in five even admitted to getting up in the middle of the night to browse the web. More significantly, argues the report, broadband is encouraging people to take a more active role online.

In our recent class discussion, we delved into the assigned reading for the week, specifically Edward S. Casey's seminal work, "Getting Back into Place  Toward a Renewed Study of the Place-World." The first chapter, "Implacement," and the subsequent "Displacement," offer a thought-provoking exploration of the concept of time as the dominant force shaping our contemporary world and culture. Casey skillfully argues that our society's emphasis on time as the situating force has led to a disconnection from the notion of place as the central axis of our cultural identity. He posits that by reconceptualizing place as the foundation of culture, we can cultivate a more grounded and community-driven existence. This reading has been the most compelling thus far in our course, and I must admit that it has left a lasting impression on me.

Before the world of humans began, there was the world of the gods, made of fields, plains and gardens. Four brothers wandered this celestial realm. They had no family other than each other— they didn’t even know who their parents were. One of the brothers, deminan, looked different from the others. His skin was covered in painful scabs, and he wondered why he alone had been marked with this affliction.one day, while the supreme spirit yaya was out in his gardens, deminan and his brothers snuck into yaya’s house. After feasting and exploring, they spotted a giant gourd hanging in the corner. But as they tried to look inside the gourd, they dropped it.

Hearts 2-1 livingston hearts wrapped up their scottish cup quarter-final tie against livingston with two goals in the first 10 minutes. Lee miller scored inside the opening 60 seconds, heading over colin meldrum and into the net from a jamie mcallister free kick. Mcallister himself scored the second when he met saul mikoliunas' cross at the back post to put the game beyond the visitors. Craig easton headed in a controversial goal after an hour but hearts held on. The opening goal came after gus bahoken had clumsily fouled miller 35 yards from goal. Mcallister floated the free kick into the livi box and miller outjumped the static visiting defence to loop his header from 10 yards over meldrum. With just 10 minutes on the clock, the home side doubled their lead. Mikoliunas sprinted down the right, crossed to the back post where mcallister raced in to head past meldrum.

Understanding how strongly correlated two-dimensional (2d) systems can give rise to unconventional superconductivity with high critical temperatures is one of the major unsolved problems in condensed matter physics. Ultracold 2d fermi gases have emerged as clean and controllable model systems to study the interplay of strong correlations and reduced dimensionality, but direct evidence of superfluidity in these systems has been missing. Here, we demonstrate superfluidity in an ultracold 2d fermi gas by moving a periodic potential through the system and observing no dissipation below a critical velocity v$_{\rm c}$. We measure v$_{\rm c}$ as a function of interaction strength and find a maximum in the crossover regime between bosonic and fermionic superfluidity. Our measurement establishes ultracold fermi gases as a powerful tool for studying the influence of reduced dimensionality on strongly correlated superfluids.

Home phones face unclear future the fixed line phone in your home could soon be an endangered species. Research by handset maker nokia shows that more and more people are using their mobile phone for every call they make or take. According to the study, more than 45 million people in the uk, germany, us and south korea now only use a mobile. It showed that people keep their fixed line phone because call charges are lower, but most of those questioned said the future was definitely mobile. The nokia-sponsored research showed that mobiles and fixed phones were used for different purposes. Home phones were used for longer calls but conversations on mobiles tended to be shorter, between mobiles and to friends. In the uk 69% of those questioned said they turned to their fixed phone because it was still cheaper to use than a mobile.

In these tumultuous economic times, I'd like to share a remarkable tale of commercial success that has largely flown under the radar. This phenomenon bears some resemblance to the achievements of industry giants like Microsoft and Google, albeit in a distinctly unconventional manner. Notably, it has defied the current recession with remarkable resilience. I am, of course, referring to the realm of organized crime. While it's true that organized crime has a long and storied history, its recent trajectory is nothing short of astonishing. Over the past two decades, it has undergone an unprecedented period of expansion, now accounting for a staggering 15% of the world's GDP. I've come to think of this vast, clandestine network as the "Global Shadow Economy" or, more colloquially, "McMafia." So, what sparked this extraordinary surge in transnational crime?

My journey to understanding the significance of birth certificates in international development began 20 years ago, when my family was in the process of adopting my two younger brothers from Ethiopia. A seemingly innocuous question posed by my mother sparked a profound realization: "What date should we use as their birthday?" My initial response, "the day they were born, of course," was met with a sobering reality check. My mother explained that neither of my brothers possessed a birth certificate, rendering their exact birthdates unknown. This revelation had a profound impact on me, and it has since evolved into a lifelong pursuit to address the global issue of missing birth certificates. Today, I am driven to explore the critical role that birth certificates play in international development, and to find innovative solutions to this complex problem.

A group of MPs has launched a detailed plan to replace the House of Lords, increasing pressure on Tony Blair for reform. This cross-party initiative has introduced a draft bill that suggests creating a smaller second chamber, with 70% of its members being elected. Since 1999, when 600 hereditary peers were removed, MPs and peers have struggled to reach an agreement on reforms. The group claims to have the support needed to eliminate the remaining 92 hereditary peers. The government had postponed their removal due to expected resistance in the Lords, stating that success seemed unlikely. Tony Blair has emphasized the need for consensus on these reforms. It has also been suggested that Labour's manifesto might include proposals to amend at least the powers of the Lords.

This paper shows that the eccentric debris rings seen around the stars fomalhaut and hd 202628 are narrower than expected in the standard eccentric planet perturbation scenario (sometimes referred to as "pericenter glow"). The standard scenario posits an initially circular and narrow belt of planetesimals at semi-major axis $a$, whose eccentricity is increased to $e_f$ after the gas disc has dispersed by secular perturbations from an eccentric planet, resulting in a belt of width $2ae_f$. In a minor modification of this scenario, narrower belts can arise if the planetesimals are initially eccentric, which could result from earlier planet perturbations during the gas-rich protoplanetary disc phase. However, a primordial eccentricity could alternatively be caused by instabilities that increase the disc eccentricity, without the need for any planets.

My journey to becoming a polar specialist and photographer began at the age of four when my family relocated from southern Canada to northern Baffin Island, near Greenland. We settled in a small Inuit community of about 200 people, and we were one of only three non-Inuit families there. In this remote area, we had no television, computers, or even a radio. We didn't have a telephone either. I spent all my time outdoors with the Inuit, playing and learning from them. The snow and ice became my playground, and the Inuit were my mentors. This experience kindled my deep fascination with the polar regions.

Scotland's Justice Minister, Cathy Jamieson, has issued a stern warning to sectarian soccer fans, vowing to take drastic measures to curb their bigoted behavior. In a bid to tackle the long-standing issue of sectarianism associated with Old Firm matches, the Minister is considering the imposition of exclusion orders, effectively banning perpetrators from attending games. This measure is part of a comprehensive campaign launched by the Scottish Executive to eradicate sectarian abuse from the sport. While acknowledging the efforts of Celtic and Rangers in addressing the problem, Ms. Jamieson emphasized that eliminating sectarian abuse remains a top priority. **Context:** The Milky Way's assembly history is now being revealed through data from the *Gaia* mission, suggesting that its globular cluster system may have undergone an equally complex formation process.  

**Aims:** This study aims to trace the formation pathway of these clusters by examining their connection to the Milky Way's documented merger events.  

**Methods:** We integrate *Gaia*'s kinematic data for nearly all galactic globular clusters with the most extensive, systematically corrected age dataset available. By analyzing their dynamical properties—particularly in integrals of motion space—we identify clusters sharing a common origin.  

**Results:** Our findings indicate that approximately 40% of the clusters likely originated within the Milky Way itself.

On October 4, 1957, the world held its breath as the Soviet Union successfully launched Sputnik, the first-ever artificial satellite. This small metal sphere, under two feet in diameter, sparked a space race between the United States and the USSR that spanned eighteen years and profoundly altered the course of history. It's worth noting, however, that Sputnik was not the first human-made object in space. That distinction belongs to the V-2 rocket, which was used by Germany to target Allied cities in the waning days of World War II—a tactic that, while not particularly effective, laid the groundwork for future space endeavors. At the war's end, both the United States and the Soviet Union seized this technology and the brilliant minds behind it, utilizing them to advance their own aerospace ambitions.

When random effects are correlated with sample design variables, the usual approach of employing individual survey weights (constructed to be inversely proportional to the unit survey inclusion probabilities) to form a pseudo-likelihood no longer produces asymptotically unbiased inference. We construct a weight-exponentiated formulation for the random effects distribution that achieves unbiased inference for generating hyperparameters of the random effects. We contrast our approach with frequentist methods that rely on numerical integration to reveal that only the bayesian method achieves both unbiased estimation with respect to the sampling design distribution and consistency with respect to the population generating distribution. Our simulations and real data example for a survey of business establishments demonstrate the utility of our approach across different modeling formulations and sampling designs. This work serves as a capstone for recent developmental efforts that combine traditional survey estimation approaches with the bayesian modeling paradigm and provides a bridge across the two rich but disparate sub-fields.

Magic fans beware: Wizards appears to be neglecting the digital gaming experience. At its core, Magic: The Gathering is a social game meant to be enjoyed with friends (or frenemies) - laughing, trash-talking, and dropping game-winning bombs. However, Wizards of the Coast has seemingly forgotten this. If you choose to play with friends, you can't earn gold, and gold is crucial. Gold unlocks everything in the game, so it's understandable that they've implemented a daily cap. But be prepared for a grueling and dull 1v1 grind against the computer or random opponents, whom you can't even speak to. If you opt for the free version, it will take you around 4 hours a day for over a month, solo, to unlock the cards.

Hapoel Bnei Sakhnin, an Israeli football club with a historic presence as the first Arab team to compete in European tournaments, has bolstered its roster with four African talents, including Zimbabwean goalkeeper Energy Murambadoro, for their upcoming UEFA Cup campaign. Their first-round fixture pits them against England’s Newcastle United, marking a significant milestone in the club’s journey.  

Murambadoro, who rose to prominence during the Africa Cup of Nations in Tunisia, played a pivotal role in Bnei Sakhnin’s dominant 6-1 victory over Albania’s Partizani Tirana in the previous qualifying round. The goalkeeper recently joined the Israeli side after a short spell with South Africa’s Hellenic FC.  

Based in the town of Sakhnin, near Haifa, the club has earned recognition not only for its on-field success—culminating in last season’s Israeli Cup triumph—but also for its unwavering commitment to fostering peace and unity within Israel’s diverse society. With a reputation built on integrity and inclusivity, Bnei Sakhnin continues to stand as a beacon of cooperation in the world of sports.

I found the concept of treating flag-burning as an offense intriguing, as it might have offered historical figures accused of witchcraft an alternative fate. As a non-American blogger, I've been hesitant to weigh in on certain debates, especially those concerning flags. However, given that I initiated the topic (though not the act of flag-burning), I should probably express my views. To begin with, I find the idea of enacting a law against flag-burning entirely irrational. When we start granting any symbol—regardless of its nature—an unquestioning reverence and legal protection, we take a significant step towards totalitarianism. Symbols are inherently ambiguous; they don’t intrinsically stand for anything. What matters is the meaning we assign to them or how they are perceived.

When developing models to summarize clinical data for simulations, it's essential to assess the models' ability to accurately replicate the original data. This can be effectively achieved through visual predictive checks (VPC). VPC involves three main steps: (1) simulating multiple reproductions of the original study using the model under evaluation, (2) calculating the estimates of interest for each of these simulated studies, and (3) comparing the distribution of these simulated estimates to the actual estimate from the original study. This approach is generally straightforward and widely applicable. Our focus here is on applying this method to time-to-event data, particularly addressing the challenge posed when a time-varying covariate is unknown or cannot be reasonably approximated after the event time.

Spontaneous conversations in real-world settings such as those found in child-centered recordings have been shown to be amongst the most challenging audio files to process. Nevertheless, building speech processing models handling such a wide variety of conditions would be particularly useful for language acquisition studies in which researchers are interested in the quantity and quality of the speech that children hear and produce, as well as for early diagnosis and measuring effects of remediation. In this paper, we present our approach to designing an open-source neural network to classify audio segments into vocalizations produced by the child wearing the recording device, vocalizations produced by other children, adult male speech, and adult female speech. To this end, we gathered diverse child-centered corpora which sums up to a total of 260 hours of recordings and covers 10 languages.

Online reviews provide product evaluations for customers to make decisions. Unfortunately, the evaluations can be manipulated using fake reviews ("spams") by professional spammers, who have learned increasingly insidious and powerful spamming strategies by adapting to the deployed detectors. Spamming strategies are hard to capture, as they can be varying quickly along time, different across spammers and target products, and more critically, remained unknown in most cases. Furthermore, most existing detectors focus on detection accuracy, which is not well-aligned with the goal of maintaining the trustworthiness of product evaluations. To address the challenges, we formulate a minimax game where the spammers and spam detectors compete with each other on their practical goals that are not solely based on detection accuracy. Nash equilibria of the game lead to stable detectors that are agnostic to any mixed detection strategies.

The pursuit of causal inference from observational data is a primary objective in many health and social science disciplines. However, the academic community has traditionally been skeptical about data analysis with a causal objective. The introduction of the term 'data science' offers a historic opportunity to redefine data analysis in a way that naturally incorporates causal inference from observational data. Like others before, we categorize the scientific contributions of data science into three distinct classes of tasks  description, prediction, and counterfactual prediction (which encompasses causal inference). A clear classification of data science tasks is essential to discuss the data, assumptions, and analytics required to successfully accomplish each task. We argue that a failure to adequately describe the role of subject-matter expert knowledge in data analysis is a root cause of widespread misconceptions about data science.

Wales 'must learn from England's health model,' new health minister says. Dr. Brian Gibbons, on his first full day in the job after Jane Hutt was sacked, acknowledged that there are lessons to be learned from the English healthcare system in tackling waiting lists. Despite facing 'big challenges,' Dr. Gibbons insisted that the 'essentials' were in place, but both Plaid Cymru and the Liberal Democrats called for policy changes. Meanwhile, Ms. Hutt defended her record, stating that waiting times and lists were only '10% of the health agenda.' Dr. Gibbons, a former GP in Blaengwynfi, in the Upper Afan Valley, before becoming AM for Aberavon, emphasized the need for a period of consolidation after 'tremendous change and reform' by NHS staff. The length of waiting lists, which had been a significant challenge for Ms. Hutt during her five-and-a-half years as the Assembly's first Health Minister, remained a major concern.

In the midst of a tumultuous era, a harrowing dilemma confronted the accused in Salem, Massachusetts, between February 1692 and May 1693. The gravity of the situation was stark  confess to a crime you didn't commit, and you might be set free; or, maintain your innocence and risk a public execution. This was the agonizing choice faced by those accused of witchcraft, who were the innocent pawns in a game of mass hysteria, fueled by superstition and misdirected religious fervor. The justice system of the time prioritized repentance over truth, often leading to devastating consequences.

Salem, settled by the Puritans in 1626, was a rigid and isolated community. The inhabitants lived in a state of constant turmoil, beset by conflicts with their Native American neighbors and rival French settlers. The Puritans' strict adherence to their beliefs and customs created an environment where the boundaries between reality and fantasy blurred, laying the groundwork for the tragic events that would unfold.

While electrons are traditionally viewed as possessing a single form of kinetic energy, could their spin, charge, or interactions with neighboring electrons introduce additional energy states? In one-dimensional systems, the Tomonaga-Luttinger liquid (TLL) model effectively describes low-energy electron interactions, though experimental evidence for nonlinear behavior remains scarce. Our study employs tunneling spectroscopy to investigate many-body excitations in gated 1D nanowires. At elevated energies, we identify distinct Fermi seas corresponding to spin and charge excitations, along with three novel 1D "replica" modes whose intensity grows inversely with wire length. By modulating inter-subband screening, we demonstrate tunable interaction strengths with variations exceeding 45%.

Yukos, an energy company, will return to a US court on Wednesday to seek sanctions against Baikal Finance Group, a little-known firm that purchased its main asset. Yukos has stated that it will sue Baikal and other parties involved in the sale of Yuganskneftegas for $20 billion in damages. Yukos' US lawyers will attempt to have Baikal's assets frozen after the Russian government ignored a US court order last week blocking the sale. The background and motives of Baikal in buying the Yuganskneftegas production unit are still unclear, with Russian newspapers suggesting that Baikal has strong ties to Surgutneftegas, Russia's fourth-largest oil producer.

Iraq is inviting bids for two telephone licenses, aiming to significantly boost nationwide coverage over the next decade. The Ministry of Communications has invited local, Arab, and international companies to submit their bids. The winning bidder will partner with the Iraqi Telecommunications and Post Company (ITPC) to install and operate a fixed phone network, providing voice, fax, and internet services. The goal is to increase Iraq's very low telephone service penetration rate from approximately 4.5% to around 25% within the next 10 years, as well as develop a highly visible and dynamic telecommunications sector. The details of the bidding and tender process will be published on the ministry's website on February 9th. Additionally, the ministry is planning a roadshow for investors in Amman, Jordan to provide further information and facilitate investment opportunities.

A novel paradigm in multimedia forensics leverages fluctuations in electric network frequency (ENF) as a potent forensic tool. This paper presents an innovative approach to ENF estimation, harnessing the filter-bank Capon spectral estimator in conjunction with temporal windowing techniques. By exploiting the Toeplitz structure inherent in the model covariance matrix, a specialized Gohberg-Semencul factorization is employed, thereby facilitating efficient computation. Notably, this methodology pioneers the use of non-rectangular temporal windows in the pre-spectral estimation stage, a departure from conventional approaches in ENF analysis. Furthermore, the incorporation of Krylov matrices enables rapid matrix inversion, significantly enhancing computational efficiency. The proposed framework demonstrates superior performance compared to existing state-of-the-art methods in ENF estimation, particularly when applied to power recordings with a short time window of 1 second. Additionally, in the context of speech recordings, this approach yields remarkably accurate results, striking a balance between computational complexity and precision.

Sshackle.nsf notice no. 00-338 october 3, 2000 memorandum to: all nymex and comex members from: neal l. Wolkoff, executive vice president date: october 3, 2000 re: notice of cftc approval of new nymex rule 6.40c: ("average price calculations by futures commission merchants") please be advised that the cftc has approved new nymex rule 6.40c ("average price calculations by futures commission merchants"), which is attached and is now in effect. New nymex rule 6.40c, which is consistent with a recent cftc "advisory," permits fcms, under certain conditions specified in the rule, to use their own internal, proprietary systems to generate average price ("ap") calculations when multiple prices are received on an order or series of orders.

El Paso Natural Gas has launched an open season to gauge market interest in a potential mainline expansion, a move that aims to alleviate the pressing capacity constraints at the California border. This development comes on the heels of a previous open season, which saw an overwhelming response with bids for 14.4 billion cubic feet per day (bcf/d) of firm transportation capacity, far exceeding the initial offering of 1.2 bcf/d. The pipeline has been facing intense scrutiny from regulators and shippers, who have been urging El Paso to increase its capacity to meet the surging demand and skyrocketing prices in California. In a recent letter, Daniel M. Adamson, Director of the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission's (FERC) Office of Energy Projects, emphasized the need for El Paso to consider modifying its Line 2000 project to help mitigate the current challenges plaguing the California gas market.
